shortDescription.text: Missing admin authorization check on benefits modification endpoint

fullDescription.text: The POST /benefits route only checks isLoggedIn but does not verify isAdmin. Any authenticated user can invoke updateBenefits, which should be restricted to administrators. This constitutes a broken function-level access control vulnerability (OWASP A01:2021).

shortDescription.text: User-controlled userId parameter allows modification of other users' benefits

fullDescription.text: The updateBenefits handler accepts userId from req.body without verifying that the authenticated user is authorized to modify that user's benefits. An attacker can supply an arbitrary userId to modify any user's benefit records. The benefitsDAO.updateBenefits function uses parseInt(userId) directly from the request body, enabling horizontal privilege escalation.

shortDescription.text: No CSRF protection on state-changing POST /benefits endpoint

fullDescription.text: The POST /benefits endpoint performs a state-changing operation (benefit modification) but has no CSRF token validation. The CSRF middleware is commented out in the application configuration, leaving all POST endpoints vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

shortDescription.text: No authorization verification on benefit data modification

fullDescription.text: The benefits modification flow from route handler through DAO layer contains no authorization check whatsoever. Authentication (isLoggedIn) is present but authorization (verifying the user has permission to modify benefits) is entirely absent. Combined with the IDOR via userId, this allows any authenticated user to escalate privileges and modify any user's benefits.

message.text: POST /benefits route uses isLoggedIn middleware but omits isAdmin check. Any authenticated user can call updateBenefits to modify benefit records, which should be an admin-only operation. Add isAdmin middleware: app.post('/benefits', isLoggedIn, isAdmin, benefitsHandler.updateBenefits).

updateBenefits handler reads userId from req.body and passes it to benefitsDAO.updateBenefits(parseInt(userId), ...) without verifying the authenticated user owns that userId. An attacker can set userId to any value in the POST body to modify another user's benefits (horizontal privilege escalation). The userId should be derived from the authenticated session, not from user-supplied input.

The POST /benefits endpoint modifies user benefit data but lacks CSRF token validation. The CSRF middleware is commented out in the application, so no state-changing endpoints are protected. An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits a forged POST request to /benefits while the victim is authenticated, silently modifying benefit records.

The entire benefits modification flow (route → handler → DAO) lacks authorization checks. Authentication via isLoggedIn confirms the user's identity but never verifies they are permitted to perform benefit modifications. This missing authorization, combined with the user-controlled userId parameter, creates a compound vulnerability allowing any authenticated user to modify any other user's benefits.
